coredata.dc:title: Scavenging activity and reaction mechanism of Ti3C2Tx MXene as a novel free radical scavenger
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.

coredata.dc:creator: Qi, Zhaojun
Wang, Sen
Li, Yan
Wang, Lifeng
Zhao, Lin
Ge, Qiang
Zhang, Jin Zhong
coredata.dc:description: A novel free radical scavenger, multi-layered Ti3C2Tx MXene (ML-Ti3C2Tx), has been studied by evaluating its scavenging activity against 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH•). It exhibits high scavenging efficiency (95% in 10 min) at low dosage (0.06 mg/mL). Based on the analysis of structure and surface states of ML-Ti3C2Tx before and after reaction with DPPH• and a series of comparative experiments, including few-layered Ti3C2Tx MXene (FL-Ti3C2Tx), original Ti3AlC2, and soluble Ti species derived from ML-Ti3C2Tx, the observed high scavenging activity is attributed to the intrinsic reducing property of ML-Ti3C2Tx rather than the hydrogen donation ability from surface functional groups. A model is proposed to explain the scavenging mechanism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 2D materials
Ti3C2Tx MXene
Free radical scavenger
Scavenging mechanism
Scavenging activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of DPPH• radical scavenging efficiency
Evaluation of scavenging activity and endogenous reducibility

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of ML-Ti3C2Tx; (b) HTEM image of ML-Ti3C2Tx; (c and d) SEM images of ML-Ti3C2Tx.
The UV–Vis spectra of DPPH• after reaction with ML-Ti3C2Tx of different concentrations and time: (a) 0.060 mg/mL, (b) 0.030 mg/mL, (c) 0.010 mg/mL.

The UV–vis spectra of DPPH incubated with 0.03 mg/mL Ti3AlC2 and H2O for (a) 1 min, (b) 10 min, (c) 30 min; DPPH• incubated with 0.6 mg/mL H–Ti3C2Tx of 200 μL for (d) 10 min, (e) 1 h, (f) 3 h; DPPH• incubated with 0.06 mg/mL FL-Ti3C2Tx of 200 μL for (g) 10 min, (h) 1 h, (i) 3 h.
(A) UV–Vis spectra of ML-Ti3C2Tx and FL-Ti3C2Tx reaction with KMnO4, (b) FT-IR spectra of Ti3AlC2, Ti3C2Tx, and Ti3C2Tx-DPPH, (c) Raman spectra of Ti3C2Tx, and Ti3C2Tx-DPPH.
Broad and high-resolution C1s, O1s XPS spectra of (a, d and g) ML-Ti3C2Tx, (b, e and h) Ti3C2Tx-DPPH, and (c, f and i) dialysate.
Proposed mechanism for the reaction of ML-Ti3C2Tx with DPPH•.
Comparison of different kinds of DPPH• scavengers with the highest scavenging efficiency.
Element content of ML-Ti3C2Tx and Ti3C2Tx-DPPH.

head.ce:title: Scavenging activity and reaction mechanism of Ti3C2Tx MXene as a novel free radical scavenger

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing, 100083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing, 100083, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, University of California, Santa Cruz, CA, 95064, United States
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, University of California, Santa Cruz, California, 95064, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel free radical scavenger, multi-layered Ti3C2Tx MXene (ML-Ti3C2Tx), has been studied by evaluating its scavenging activity against 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH•). It exhibits high scavenging efficiency (95% in 10 min) at low dosage (0.06 mg/mL). Based on the analysis of structure and surface states of ML-Ti3C2Tx before and after reaction with DPPH• and a series of comparative experiments, including few-layered Ti3C2Tx MXene (FL-Ti3C2Tx), original Ti3AlC2, and soluble Ti species derived from ML-Ti3C2Tx, the observed high scavenging activity is attributed to the intrinsic reducing property of ML-Ti3C2Tx rather than the hydrogen donation ability from surface functional groups. A model is proposed to explain the scavenging mechanism.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to unpaired electrons, free radicals are highly reactive. When free radicals are produced in organisms, often in the form of reactive oxygen species (ROS), they can cause pathological changes [1]. In order to reduce or inhibit the oxidative damage caused by ROS, free radical scavengers are often used to reduce their concentration in the body by electron or hydrogen donation or adduct formation [2]. To date, several natural radical scavengers have been extracted from foods and plants, which are safe for organisms but usually low in scavenging activity [3,4]. Synthetic radical scavengers are more effective, but most have side effects, which can be manifested in carcinogenesis, teratogenesis, and mutagenicity [5]. Therefore, there is a strong interest in the design and development of new non-invasive radical scavengers.

Recently, a new type of multifunctional transition metal carbon/nitride material has been discovered that augments the family of two-dimensional (2D) materials [6]. Similar to the structure of graphene, this new layered ultrathin nanosheets has been referred as “MXenes”. The general formula is Mn+1XnTx (n = 1–3), where the M represents the front transition metal element (e.g., Sc, Ti, V, Cr, Zr, Nb, etc.), the X is C or N, and the Tx indicates –OH, –O, –F and other surface hydrophilic groups [7]. Ti3C2Tx MXene was first peeled off from the bulk MAX phase by Al removal [6]. Metallic conductivity and hydrophilic surface are two of the unique properties of Ti3C2Tx MXene compared to other 2D materials. Together with excellent mechanical properties, Ti3C2Tx MXene has become a promising alternative material for energy storage, separation membranes, catalysis, electromagnetic, and shielding [8–13]. Importantly, Ti3C2Tx MXene is safe for common tissue cells [14] and aquatic biological cells [15], and has been reported to be used as a photothermal converter in organisms [16,17]. Its low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility in a living organism are highly desirable. Moreover, Ti3C2Tx MXene can be easily oxidized into TiO2 and CO2 in the presence of water or copresence of water and oxygen [18,19]. This is potentially useful for reducing free radicals, an area yet to be explored.

In this work, multi-layered Ti3C2Tx MXene (ML-Ti3C2Tx) was used as a free radical scavenger for the first time. 1,1-diphenyl-2-pycryl (DPPH•), a stable free radical [6,12] was used to evaluate the scavenging activity of Ti3C2Tx. ML-Ti3C2Tx exhibited high scavenging efficiency and low usage towards DPPH•. Through a series of comparative experiments including few-layered Ti3C2Tx MXene (FL-Ti3C2Tx), original Ti3AlC2, and soluble Ti species derived from ML-Ti3C2Tx, and the detailed analysis of the structure and surface state changes before and after the scavenging reaction, the inherent reducibility is considered as mainly responsible for the high scavenging efficiency.

HF etching was used to synthesize ML-Ti3C2Tx. 10 g Ti3AlC2 powder was added gradually to 100 mL of 50 wt% HF while stirring. The gradual addition is to avoid overheating and excessive bubbling. The reaction was then allowed to proceed at room temperature for 18 h. The ML-Ti3C2Tx sediment was collected via centrifugation, and then washed several times using deionized water until the pH of the supernatant became ~6. Finally, the ML-Ti3C2Tx powders were dried in a vacuum at room temperature for 24 h.

1.0 g ML-Ti3C2Tx powder was dissolved in 15 mL of DMSO and continuously stirred for 24 h. After the intercalation reaction, DMSO was removed with deionized water, washed several times at the speed of 5000 r/min, and dried in vacuum at room temperature (25 °C). Then, 1.0 g of this powder dissolved in 500 mL deionized water and ultrasonic treatment for 4–6 h. Next, the solution was centrifuged at 3500 r/min for 1 h, and the upper dark green aqueous solution was observed. The FL-Ti3C2Tx powder was finally obtained by vacuum-assisted suction filtration and vacuum-dried this dark green clear aqueous solution at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of DPPH• radical scavenging efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: DPPH• is a type of relatively stable radical and often used as a model to assess the scavenging activity of compounds. DPPH• can combine with H or accept electron from the scavenger, and the reaction between DPPH• and a radical scavenger yields the reduced and stable DPPH• complex, accompanied by a color change from deep purple to pale yellow. DPPH• shows strong absorption at 515 nm in the UV–Vis spectrum, and scavenging efficiency could be determined by the drop of absorbance at 515 nm. Radical scavenging activity (RSA) is calculated by the equation RSA(%)= (A-A1)/A × 100%.

The scavenging activity of ML-Ti3C2Tx was conducted as below. 200 μL aqueous solutions containing different amount (0.020 mg, 0.06 mg, 0.12 mg) of ML-Ti3C2Tx was separately dropped into 2.0 mL 0.050 mg/mL DPPH• freshly prepared with ethanol. The final concentrations of ML-Ti3C2Tx in the mixed solution are 0.010 mg/mL, 0.030 mg/mL, and 0.060 mg/mL. After 5 seconds of ultrasonication, the mixtures were left in the dark at room temperature, and the optical absorption at 515 nm was measured after 1 min, 3 min, 10 min, 30 min, 1 h and 3 h, respectively. The RSA is calculated according the above equation. The reactive product is named as Ti3C2Tx-DPPH.

Acid potassium permanganate (KMnO4) was used to evaluate the endogenous reducibility of ML-Ti3C2Tx and FL-Ti3C2Tx. KMnO4 and ML-Ti3C2Tx or FL-Ti3C2Tx solution are mixed and placed in a dark environment for 30 min. The concentration of KMnO4 in the mixed solution was 100 μM, ML-Ti3C2Tx and FL-Ti3C2Tx were both 30 μM. Then the absorption peak at 515 nm of KMnO4 was measured using the UV–Vis spectra.

X-ray diffraction was measured using a TTR III multifunction X-ray diffractometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained using H-7650B and FlexSEM1000 electron microscope, respectively. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured using a UNICUV-2800 spectrophotometer. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured using a NEXUS spectrometer 670. Raman spectra were recorded using an RM 2000 Microscopic Confocal Raman Spectrometer (Renishaw PLC, England) under a He–Ne laser with an excitation wavelength of 632.8 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using an ESCALAB 250Xi electron spectrometer equipped with an Al monochromatic Ka radiation (hυ = 1486.6 eV) source. The C1s, O1s spectra were fitted using the XPS data, which were obtained by deconvolution of the spectra using the software program XPS Peak 4.0. All the characterizations for samples after scavenging reaction were conducted after dialyzing for 12 h.

The XRD patterns of Ti3AlC2 and ML-Ti3C2Tx were measured, as shown in Fig. 1 a. Ti3AlC2 exhibits typical diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 9.5°, 19.1°, 34.0°, 36.7°, 39.0°, 41.8°, 44.9°, 48.5°, 52.4° and 56.5°, corresponding to the (002), (004), (101), (103), (104), (105), (106), (107), (108) and (109) planes of Ti3AlC2, respectively. After 18 h etching, the characteristic peaks at (101) to (109) disappeared, demonstrating the full removal of the Al layer [20]. Besides, the (002) and (004) diffraction peaks move to low angles and tend to broaden, indicating the increase in layer spacing by Al removal. The layer spacing is calculated to be about 18 Å by the Bragg formula. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1b) exhibits clear lattice fringes, with lattice spacing of ~0.261 nm, corresponding to the (006) plane of ML-Ti3C2Tx. The corresponding selected area diffraction (SAD; inset in Fig. 1b) pattern shows clear hexagonal symmetry of the planes, demonstrating ML-Ti3C2Tx maintains the hexagonal structure of the original Ti3AlC2 phase after Al removing. Fig. 1c and d shows the SEM images of the ML-Ti3C2Tx, where cross sectional shear slip appears and a typical accordion-like morphology is observed, further providing evidence for the successful exfoliation of Ti3AlC2 and large interlayer spacing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of scavenging activity and endogenous reducibility
ce:section.ce:para: DPPH• was used to investigate the scavenging activity of ML-Ti3C2Tx. After different reaction times, the color of all DPPH• solutions changed from purple to yellow, as shown in the photos as inset in Fig. 2 . The absorption intensity for the peak at λ max = 515 nm of DPPH• in the UV–Vis spectra decreases rapidly with reaction time. The higher the concentration of ML-Ti3C2Tx, the shorter the reaction time. The scavenging efficiency can reach 95% in 10 min for the sample with 0.060 mg/mL, as well as in 1 h and 3 h for the sample with 0.030 mg/mL and 0.010 mg/mL. This high scavenging efficiency and short reaction time are significantly superior to most 2D materials, and one-dimensional (1D) and zero-dimensional (0D) carbon materials reported as scavengers. As summarized in Table 1 [21–27], the DPPH• radical scavenging efficiency of 2D materials, such as g-C3N4 (0.10 mg/mL), MoS2 (0.30 mg/mL) and GO (1.0 mg/mL), was 45%, 70%, 15%, respectively. The reaction time was 30 min, 6 min and 680 min, with the concentrations of DPPH• at 0.10 mg/mL, 0.39 mg/mL, 0.12 mg/mL, respectively. 1D carbon nanotubes (CNT) with concentration of 0.40 mg/mL can scavenge 45.5% DPPH• of 0.060 mg/mL. Although the efficiency can reach 99.5% using carboxyl modified CNT, the reaction time was up to 10 h [23]. 0D carbon dots (CDs) with a concentration of 0.16 mg/mL can scavenge 87% DPPH• (0.060 mg/mL) in 5 min, but further addition of CDs cannot scavenge more DPPH• [25]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with a concentration of 0.050 mg/mL also show scavenging activity for DPPH• (0.050 mg/mL), but the scavenging efficiency is lower than 70% with the reaction time of 15 h [26]. When the reaction lasted five days, the scavenging efficiency increased to 80% [27].

To confirm that the scavenging activity is originated from ML-Ti3C2Tx rather than self-reduction of DPPH• or possible effect of trace amounts of Ti3AlC2 remaining after etching or soluble Ti species derived from ML-Ti3C2Tx causing by the decomposition, a series of controlled experiments were conducted. First, the scavenging activity of Ti3AlC2 and deionized water were carried out. 200 μL of 0.30 mg/mL Ti3AlC2 and deionized water were separately added into 2.0 mL of 0.050 mg/mL DPPH• ethanol solution, the mixed solutions were left in the dark after 5 min of ultrasonication. As shown in Fig. 3 a–c, the absorption peak of DPPH• at λ max = 515 nm is essentially unchanged for the two samples after 30 min. These results demonstrate that DPPH• is a stable free radical in water and Ti3AlC2 has no scavenging activity against DPPH•.

Second, the ML-Ti3C2Tx aqueous solution with 0.60 mg/mL was heated at 60 °C for 1 h to promote the formation of soluble Ti species (named H–Ti3C2Tx) from Ti3C2Tx decomposition. After reaction, a clear and colourless solution was obtained after filtration. As shown in Fig. 3d–f, the absorbance of DPPH• did not change significantly even reaction with this obtained solution for 3 h. It indicated that the high scavenging activity of ML-Ti3C2Tx to DPPH• did not come from the active Ti species after ML-Ti3C2Tx decomposition. Here, it is believed that the slight decrease in absorbance may be caused by the presence of tiny ML-Ti3C2Tx flakes (diameter < 220 nm) in the obtained solution.

Considering the scavenging reaction may take place at the surface or the edges, FL-Ti3C2Tx prepared by DMSO intercalation with a large surface area and high density of surface functional groups was used to test its scavenging activity. The TEM image of Fig. S1 showed its single layer structure and the XPS results of Fig. S2 reveal a large amount of surface functional groups of FL-Ti3C2Tx. However, the scavenging activity of FL-Ti3C2Tx towards DPPH• was low, just 33% dropping in absorbance in the first 3 h (in Fig. 3g–i). This result demonstrated that although a large specific surface area can enhance contact between DPPH• and Ti3C2Tx, hydrogen donating from surface functional groups is not the reason of high scavenging activity of ML-Ti3C2Tx.

However, ML-Ti3C2Tx exhibited strong endogenous reducibility, as shown in the reaction with KMnO4. In Fig. 4 a, the original KMnO4 solution showed multiple prominent absorption peaks within the range of 450 nm–600 nm, but after reaction with ML-Ti3C2Tx in the dark for 30 min, all the absorption peaks within this range disappeared, and the solution also changed from purple to light yellow, indicating the reduction of Mn (VII) to Mn (IV). Compared to the ML-Ti3C2Tx, FL-Ti3C2Tx with the same concentration and reaction time exhibited high absorption of residual KMnO4. It demonstrated that FL-Ti3C2Tx with high content of surface functional groups had weak reduction ability.

To better understand the origin of the high scavenging activity, ML-Ti3C2Tx solution with 0.030 mg/mL was chosen for FT-IR, Raman, and XPS studies before and after reaction to investigate the changes in structure and surface state. In the FT-IR spectrum of ML-Ti3C2Tx (Fig. 4b), Ti–O, CO, –OH vibrational modes are observed [28], demonstrating that oxygen-related hydrophilic groups present in the sample. However, after the reaction with DPPH•, the vibration peaks of Ti–O and –OH disappeared. This implies that scavenging of DPPH• goes through a series of chemical reactions. Moreover, the Raman spectrum revealed its structural changes after reaction. From the Raman spectrum (Fig. 4c), ML-Ti3C2Tx showed three peaks at about 265, 410, and 620 cm−1, which could all be attributed to Ti3C2Tx MXene [29,30]. These vibrational modes all disappeared after DPPH• scavenging. Two new vibration modes at approximately 1346 cm−1 (D-band) and 1587 cm−1 (G-band) appeared, which are related to the sp3-hybridized carbon and E2g phonon of sp2 carbon atoms, respectively [31]. This result indicates that the intrinsic C–Ti skeleton of Ti3C2Tx was destroyed under the attack of DPPH•, carbon layer similar graphene structure was retained after reaction.

The XPS spectrum further indicates the structure and surface functional group changes of ML-Ti3C2Tx before and after reaction. First, from the survey spectra, ML-Ti3C2Tx (Fig. 5 a) exhibits strong peaks of C1s at 284.1 eV, O1s at 531.4 eV, Ti2p at 454.3 eV, F1s at 684.4 eV. Weak Al 2p peak with trace amounts of 2.68% in Table 2 may be due to residual small sized Ti3AlC2. After the reaction with DPPH•, the peaks of Ti2p and F1s disappeared (Fig. 5b), but an N-related peak appeared, and the relative content of carbon and oxygen increased from 67.84% to 10.32%–84.79% and 11.83% in Ti3C2Tx-DPPH, respectively. This result agrees with the above Raman result, further confirming the broken of intrinsic C–Ti skeleton of Ti3C2Tx after DPPH• scavenging. In order to further determine where Ti is and the change of its valence state, we collected and concentrated dialysate (drying by vacuum box below 50 °C could shorten the drying time and avoid oxidation). The XPS result of dialysate in Fig. 5c and Table 2 show small amount of Ti and a large amount of F, indicating that Ti participated in the reaction with DPPH• and form new small size substances.

Furthermore, the C1s spectra of Ti3C2Tx (Fig. 5d) can be divided into the C–C bonds at around 284.2 eV and ML-Ti3C2Tx (Tx is mainly –O, –OH, and –F) bonds at around 281.2 eV [32,33]. In Ti3C2Tx-DPPH, the C–Ti-Tx bonds disappeared, the intensities of the C–C and CO bonds have increased, and new bonds corresponding to C–O and C–N show up in the Ti3C2Tx-DPPH (Fig. 5e). Therefore, after the breaking of the intrinsic C–Ti skeleton, new surface groups or adducts were formed in the retained graphene structure carbon layer. For the O1s fitted spectra, Ti mainly exists in the form of C–Ti–OH, C–Ti–O, and Ti–O–Ti in Ti3C2Tx (Fig. 5g), but these bonds are replaced by CO, C–O, and OC–O in the Ti3C2Tx-DPPH (Fig. 5h). Moreover, the appearance of Ti4+ in the dialysate (Fig. 5i) demonstrates that the Ti is converted to small sized TiO2 after the C–Ti skeleton broken.

Based on change in structure and surface states and analysis of all the results, we propose that strong endogenous reducibility is the main reason of the high scavenging activity for ML-Ti3C2Tx. The detailed scavenging mechanism of ML-Ti3C2Tx against DPPH• is illustrated in Fig. 6 . ML-Ti3C2Tx has an accordion-like morphology and large interlayer spacing that allow DPPH• to insert into the space of layers and ensures good contact of DPPH• with Ti3C2Tx layers. There are three chemical bonds in the original ML-Ti3C2Tx, Ti–C, Ti–F, and Ti–OH. Among them, the Ti–C bond is the most active, because C has the least electronegativity (C-2.55, O-3.44, F-3.98) [34], and Ti–C bond has the lowest dissociation energy (Ti–C 435 ± 25 D/kJ mol−1, Ti–F 569 ± 34 D/kJ mol−1, Ti–O 662 ± 16 D/kJ mol−1) [35]. DPPH• can break most of the C–O bonds in GQDs [36,37]. Under the attack of DPPH•, intrinsic Ti–C bonds of ML-Ti3C2Tx are first to break, resulting in layered C and single Ti ions. Then, the surface defects of layered C atoms are occupied by DPPH• and oxygen-containing functional groups in water. Thus, DPPH• loses activity by adduct formation with layered C. The Ti ions react with dissolved oxygen to form small-sized TiO2. This primary process for DPPH• scavenging can explain the structural and surface group changes of ML-Ti3C2Tx as observed in FT-IR, Raman, and XPS.

In summary, ML-Ti3C2Tx was synthesized by HF acid etching and its scavenging activity was investigated for the first time. High scavenging efficiency against DPPH• has been found with performance superior to most of previously reported scavengers based on 2D materials and 1D or 0D carbon materials. By analyzing changes in the structure and surface states of ML-Ti3C2Tx before and after the reaction with DPPH•, the scavenging mechanism was revealed. The intrinsic reducing property of ML-Ti3C2Tx rather than the hydrogen donation ability from surface functional groups is found to be primarily responsible for observed high scavenging efficiency. The new insight gained into the scavenging mechanism of Ti3C2Tx will help the design and development of MXene materials for antioxidation applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21674011) and Beijing Municipal Natural Science Foundation (2172040).
